nlp: The Anatoly pipeline processes TypeScript projects through seven sequential phases: scan, estimate, triage, RAG index, review, deliberation, and report. Each phase is crash-isolated so that a failure in one axis does not prevent the others from producing results. The scan phase uses tree-sitter WASM to parse every TypeScript file into an AST and extract symbol metadata including function names, class names, exported constants, and their line ranges.

The deliberation pass is the quality gate that separates Anatoly from simple linters. After all seven axes have independently evaluated a file, their results are merged into a single ReviewFile by the axis-merger. If any symbol has findings (NEEDS_FIX, ERROR, DEAD, DUPLICATE, or OVER), the merged review is sent to a deliberation agent powered by Claude Opus. The deliberation agent reviews each finding with full source code context and can reclassify, confirm, or remove findings. This two-tier approach (fast collection by Haiku/Sonnet, then verification by Opus) maximizes both recall and precision.

The RAG engine provides semantic search capabilities for the duplication and documentation axes. It maintains a LanceDB vector database with two types of entries: function cards (code embeddings for duplication detection) and documentation sections (NLP embeddings for doc matching). In advanced mode, function code is embedded via nomic-embed-code and NLP summaries via Qwen3-Embedding-8B, both served through Docker llama.cpp containers using GGUF quantized models. The dual-vector architecture enables hybrid search combining code similarity and semantic similarity with configurable weighting.

The correction axis uses a two-pass approach to minimize false positives. In Pass 1, a Sonnet model evaluates each symbol for bugs, logic errors, async issues, and API misuse. If Pass 1 flags any issues and the file imports external dependencies, Pass 2 automatically fires a verification query that includes targeted README sections from node_modules for the implicated libraries. Findings contradicted by library documentation are reclassified to OK and recorded in the deliberation memory so they are not re-flagged in future runs.

The usage graph is a directed graph tracking import relationships between TypeScript files. It is built during the setup phase by scanning all import statements, re-exports, and dynamic imports. The graph enables the utility axis to determine whether exported symbols are actually imported by other files in the project. It also tracks transitive intra-file references: if function A calls function B in the same file, and A is imported externally, B is marked as transitively USED rather than DEAD.

The test axis evaluates the quality of existing test coverage, not just its presence. For each source file, it resolves the corresponding test file (foo.test.ts or foo.spec.ts) and injects its content into the evaluation prompt. The LLM analyzes whether tests cover behavioral scenarios, edge cases, and error paths rather than just achieving line coverage. It also receives the symbol's callers from the usage graph to understand the business context. This enables detection of over-mocking, trivial assertions, and missing integration tests.

The best practices axis evaluates files against 17 TypeScript-specific rules organized by severity (CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM). Critical rules include 'no any type' which detects JSON.parse, API responses, and function parameters typed as any, and 'security' which checks for command injection via template literals in execSync, SQL injection patterns, and XSS vectors in string concatenation. Each rule produces PASS, WARN, or FAIL with a detailed explanation. The axis is context-aware: it detects file type (test, config, React component, API handler) and adjusts rule application accordingly.

Reports are generated as markdown documents split into shards of 10 files each to keep each document focused and actionable. The index report contains a global verdict, axis summary statistics, and a checklist of all shards. Each shard contains a findings table showing all seven axes per symbol, detailed actions categorized as quickwin or refactor with severity and effort estimates, a best practices score breakdown, and a tests section with one checkbox per file that needs test improvements. The shard format is designed for consumption by the clean loop which automatically fixes findings.

Anatoly maintains a calibration system that records per-axis median durations from previous runs in a calibration.json file. This historical data drives the dry-run cost and time estimates shown in the pipeline summary before a full run begins. The estimate accounts for each phase independently: scan, estimate, triage, RAG index, review (with per-axis parallel timing), deliberation (sequential on files with findings), and report. Concurrency efficiency is factored at 0.75 to account for rate limits, API contention, and tail effects.

The overengineering axis detects Not Invented Here (NIH) patterns by receiving the project dependency metadata in its evaluation prompt. When a file imports a library like lodash or ramda, the axis checks whether any function in the file reimplements functionality already available in the installed dependency. It also evaluates general complexity: excessive abstraction layers, premature generalization, configuration-driven code that only has one configuration, and wrapper functions that add no value over calling the underlying API directly.
